jazz.story: Jazz took shape in the United States around the turn of the 20th century, most closely tied to New Orleans, and among its pioneers were [[Louis Armstrong]], [[Jelly Roll Morton]], [[King Oliver]], and [[Duke Ellington]]. The US remains the deepest well in our data, with 4819 artists and a peak in the 1940s. From there it crossed the Atlantic, with early European figures like [[Django Reinhardt]] and [[Sidney Bechet]] helping it settle in France and Britain. Later waves surfaced across Germany, France, Britain, and Japan, most clustering in the 1970s, while Norway's registered artists peak later in the 1990s.

blues.story: Blues took shape in the American South around the turn of the 20th century, growing from work songs, spirituals, and field hollers rather than any single inventor. Among its pioneers were [[W. C. Handy]], [[Ma Rainey]], [[Bessie Smith]], and [[Charley Patton]], with later figures like [[Robert Johnson]] and [[Muddy Waters]] shaping its sound. The US accounts for some 1,500 registered blues artists, and the music spread north with the Great Migration and abroad after the war. Britain caught the fire in the 1960s, its peak decade with 133 artists, while Canada, Australia, France, and Germany each grew smaller but lasting scenes.

folk.story: Folk grew from older oral and roots traditions, but its modern form took shape in the US, where among its pioneers were [[Woody Guthrie]], [[Lead Belly]], and [[Pete Seeger]], with [[Joan Baez]] and [[Bob Dylan]] carrying it further. It spread to Britain, home to key early figures like [[Ewan MacColl]], and our dataset counts 631 UK artists against 1347 in the US. From there it reached Canada, Germany, and France, each seeing steady growth into the 2000s. The numbers only reflect registered artists, but they trace a genre that traveled widely while staying close to its plain-spoken roots.

reggae.story: Reggae grew out of Jamaica in the 1960s, evolving from ska and rocksteady, with 272 JM artists in the dataset and a peak of 95 in that decade. Among its pioneers were [[Toots and the Maytals]], [[Jimmy Cliff]], [[Desmond Dekker]], and the Wailers of [[Bob Marley]], [[Peter Tosh]], and [[Bunny Wailer]], with producer [[Lee "Scratch" Perry]] shaping the sound. It reached Britain next, peaking in the 1970s (31 of 120 artists), then spread to the US and France, which each saw later booms. Smaller scenes later took root in Germany and Argentina.

punk.story: Punk took shape in the mid-1970s on both sides of the Atlantic, and its origins are lightly contested between New York and London. Among its US pioneers were [[Ramones]], [[Patti Smith]], and the earlier proto-punk of [[The Stooges]], while key early British figures included [[Sex Pistols]] and [[The Clash]], with Britain's 1970s peak of 314 registered artists. From there it spread widely: the US logged the most artists overall (1607), and scenes later took root in Germany, Sweden, France, and Japan, each with a couple hundred acts of their own.

metal.story: Metal took shape around 1970, with British and American bands among its pioneers: [[Black Sabbath]], [[Deep Purple]], and [[Judas Priest]] are the widely-agreed key figures, with [[Led Zeppelin]] often named too. The US grew the largest scene, some 3381 artists over time, peaking in the 2000s. It spread hard across Germany, where [[Scorpions]] and later thrash acts took hold, giving 1419 artists overall. Scandinavia became a stronghold too, with Sweden's death and Finland's melodic scenes each fielding roughly a thousand acts, showing how far the genre traveled.

hip hop.story: Hip hop took shape in 1970s New York, and among its pioneers were [[DJ Kool Herc]], [[Afrika Bambaataa]], and [[Grandmaster Flash]], soon joined by voices like [[Run-DMC]]. The US remains the deepest well in our dataset, with 2148 tagged artists and a 1990s peak of 694. From there it crossed the Atlantic and found strong local scenes: France registers 764 artists (peaking in the 1990s with early figures like [[IAM]] and [[MC Solaar]]), and Germany 419. Britain's 273 artists lean later, peaking in the 2000s, while Canada and Italy round out the map.

techno.story: Techno took shape in Detroit in the 1980s, where key early figures like [[Juan Atkins]], [[Derrick May]], and [[Kevin Saunderson]] built a machine-driven sound from funk and electronics; our data logs 103 US artists, peaking that decade. It crossed to Europe fast, finding a second home in Germany, where [[Sven Väth]] helped it swell to a 1990s peak of 73 artists. Britain joined the surge with figures like [[Richie Hawtin]] active across borders, reaching 153 UK artists overall. From there it spread to the Netherlands, Japan, and Italy, each adding local color to a genre with contested but shared roots.

bossa nova.story: Bossa nova took shape in late-1950s Brazil, which still accounts for most of its registered artists (68, peaking in the 1950s). Among its pioneers were [[João Gilberto]], whose soft guitar and hushed voice set the tone, alongside composer [[Antônio Carlos Jobim]] and poet-lyricist [[Vinícius de Moraes]]. It spread quickly to the United States, where [[Stan Getz]] and singer [[Astrud Gilberto]] helped carry it to wider audiences. From there the sound drifted further, later finding a steady home in Japan, which shows a small revival of about a dozen artists into the 2000s.

k-pop.story: K-pop took shape in South Korea, which anchors the genre with over 1,100 registered artists and a clear peak in the 2010s of 506. Among its pioneers, [[Seo Taiji and Boys]] mixed rap and rock into Korean pop in the early 1990s, while [[H.O.T.]], [[S.E.S.]], and [[g.o.d]] helped define the idol-group template that followed. Later acts like [[BoA]] and [[TVXQ]] pushed the sound outward, first into Japan and across Asia. The dataset shows small clusters emerging abroad too, with a handful of artists in the US, Japan, and China, though the numbers there stay modest.

electronic.story: Electronic music is the broadest lens here — an umbrella that took shape in the 1970s as synthesizers left the laboratory, with [[Kraftwerk]], [[Tangerine Dream]], [[Wendy Carlos]], and [[Giorgio Moroder]] among its pioneers across Germany, the US, and Italy. Cheap gear and home studios then spread it everywhere at once. Our dataset counts over 3,500 tagged artists, led by the US (762) and Britain (687), both peaking in the 2000s as production software made everyone a potential producer.

house.story: House was born on Chicago dance floors in the early 1980s, built from disco records and drum machines by pioneers including [[Frankie Knuckles]], [[Ron Hardy]], [[Larry Heard]], and [[Marshall Jefferson]]. Britain caught it fast — the 1988 acid-house summer made it a UK institution, and our dataset now counts Britain (281 artists) neck and neck with the US (280). Germany, France, and the Netherlands built their own 1990s scenes that never really ended.

soul.story: Soul emerged in the 1950s United States as gospel fervor met rhythm and blues, with [[Ray Charles]], [[Sam Cooke]], and [[James Brown]] among its architects and [[Aretha Franklin]] soon its defining voice. Motown, Stax, and Muscle Shoals industrialized the feeling in the 1960s. The US dominates our dataset — 1,572 of 2,600 artists, anchored in that founding era — while Britain's 310, peaking in the 2000s, trace the northern-soul obsession and the neo-soul revival that followed.

country.story: Country grew out of the folk songs, church music, and string bands of the American South, put on record in the 1920s by early figures like [[The Carter Family]] and [[Jimmie Rodgers]]. Nashville turned it into an industry, and the US still holds 1,329 of our 1,769 tagged artists, peaking in the 1990s arena era. Canada and Australia run their own traditions — and the dataset's scattering across Norway and Britain shows how far the twang carries.

salsa.story: Salsa's roots are genuinely contested — Cuban son and mambo supplied the engine, but the sound was assembled in New York's Latin neighborhoods in the late 1960s and 70s, with [[Willie Colón]], [[Héctor Lavoe]], [[Celia Cruz]], and [[Rubén Blades]] among the figures who defined it on Fania Records. Our dataset's geography tells that spread: Puerto Rico leads (69 artists, peaking in the 1970s), with Colombia's Cali scene close behind (55, peaking in the 1980s) and Cuba and Venezuela carrying their own lines.

afrobeat.story: Afrobeat was forged in 1960s-70s Lagos, where [[Fela Kuti]] and drummer [[Tony Allen]] fused highlife, jazz, and funk into long-form political groove. Nigeria still anchors the lens with 76 of our 210 tagged artists — peaking in the 2010s, when a new generation and bands abroad like [[Antibalas]] revived the form and the closely-named afrobeats wave brought new attention. Ghana, Britain, and the US carry the diaspora's share.

ambient.story: Ambient coalesced in the 1970s between Britain and Germany: [[Brian Eno]] named it and framed its intent with Music for Airports, while kosmische travelers like [[Tangerine Dream]] and [[Cluster]] had been drifting there already. Britain's 254 dataset artists peak in the 1980s, closest to the founding moment; the US total (325) peaks in the 2000s, when laptops made stillness a global pursuit. Over 1,500 artists carry the tag across 60-plus countries.

flamenco.story: Flamenco is far older than recording — it grew over centuries among the Roma communities of Andalusia, and Spain holds 91 of our 130 tagged artists, the deepest home-country concentration of any lens here. The dataset peaks in the 1960s, the recorded era of masters like [[Paco de Lucía]] and [[Camarón de la Isla]], with earlier giants like [[La Niña de los Peines]] preserved on shellac. The small numbers abroad mark emigres and devoted students, not a second home.

bluegrass.story: Bluegrass takes its very name from [[Bill Monroe]]'s Blue Grass Boys, the 1940s Kentucky band where [[Earl Scruggs]]' banjo rolls and [[Lester Flatt]]'s guitar runs set the template. It stayed strikingly American: 269 of our 302 tagged artists are US-based, peaking in the 1950s founding wave. The handful abroad — Canada, Britain, Japan — reflect one of the world's most devoted small scenes.

drum and bass.story: Drum and bass accelerated out of Britain's early-90s jungle and rave scenes, with [[Goldie]], [[LTJ Bukem]], [[Roni Size]], and [[4hero]] among the pioneers who stretched breakbeats into something both furious and elegant. Britain still owns the lens — 209 of our 482 tagged artists, peaking in the 1990s. The 2000s and 2010s took it global, with the US, Russia, and the Netherlands building scenes of their own.
